title: Bundled solar dyeing
subtitle: Using sunlight and bundled plant material to transfer colour and print onto fabric
excerpt: Solar bundle dyeing wraps fabric with fresh plant material and a mordant, seals the bundle, and places it in direct sunlight for several days to several weeks. Heat from the sun drives the dye from the plant into the fabric without a hob or dye pot. The results are unpredictable and variable: plant shapes transfer partially, colours shift as the bundle dries, and each piece is unique. This is a low-equipment process suited to a summer garden.
sourceNotes: Solar bundle dyeing is a contemporary natural dyeing practice. Technique is observation-led.

glossaryTerms.definition: A dyeing method that uses sunlight rather than a stove as the heat source. Fibre, plant material, and water are sealed in a jar or bundle and left in the sun for days to weeks.

A surface design technique in which fresh plant material is pressed directly against fabric and bundled tightly, transferring the plant's tannins and pigments as a contact print. Solar dyeing is a variation of eco-printing.

content.text: , the bundle replaces the dye pot. Plant material is pressed against mordanted fabric, rolled or folded, wrapped tightly, and left in sunlight. The heat from direct sun is sufficient to transfer plant pigments over several days. The result is closer to an

than a solid colour: leaves and flowers leave partial impressions, and colour varies from bundle to bundle. Wool and silk take the most colour; cotton requires a tannin pre-mordant to pick up plant pigments.

items.name: Mordanted wool, silk, or pre-tannined cotton fabric, damp
Fresh plant material: leaves, flowers, onion skins, rust-bearing items
items.qty: enough to cover the fabric surface

items.name: Alum solution or iron modifier liquid (for dampening layers)
String or rubber bands (to tie the bundle)
Cling film or plastic bag (to seal the bundle)

content.text: Step 1: Lay out the fabric and plant material

Lay the damp, mordanted fabric flat. Arrange plant material directly on the fabric surface. Plants high in tannins (oak leaves, ferns, eucalyptus) transfer most reliably. Fresh flowers such as marigold, coreopsis, or chamomile give yellow and gold tones. Onion skins produce orange. Rusty items placed inside the bundle add grey-brown tones from the iron.
Step 2: Roll and secure the bundle

Roll the fabric tightly with the plant material inside, pressing firmly as you roll. Tie the bundle at several points with string. Wrap the tied bundle in cling film or seal in a plastic bag to retain moisture during the solar process. The moisture is essential: dry bundles do not transfer colour.

Place the wrapped bundle in direct sunlight on a dark surface (a tray or dark cloth absorbs more heat). Leave for 3 to 7 days in summer sun, turning the bundle daily. Longer exposure deepens the colour. The bundle will warm considerably in direct sun; this warmth is the driving force for dye transfer.

Open the bundle outdoors: decomposing plant material has a strong smell. Remove plant debris and rinse the fabric in cool water. Do not scrub: the surface impression is delicate. Hang to dry out of direct sunlight. Solar-dyed fabric fades more quickly than mordant-dye-bath pieces and is best used for decorative rather than hard-wearing items.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Two common problems with solar bundle dyeing.

items.symptom: Little or no colour transfer after several days
items.cause: Bundle dried out in the sun, or plant material was not fresh.
items.fix: Re-moisten the bundle and re-wrap. Use only fresh or freshly dried plant material. The bundle must stay moist throughout the process.

items.symptom: Strong smell when opening the bundle
items.cause: Normal decomposition of plant material during extended solar processing.
items.fix: Open outdoors. The smell dissipates on rinsing. This is expected rather than a sign of failure.
